key_departures.impact: Phillips was Philadelphia's top pass rusher after being acquired from Miami at the 2025 trade deadline. In eight games with the Eagles he posted two sacks and 34 quarterback pressures. The Eagles were reportedly willing to offer $20-25M/yr but Carolina pushed to $30M/yr — a price Philly would not match. With Phillips gone, Nolan Smith Jr. and Jalyx Hunt are the only edge rushers under contract who were on the 2025 active roster.

Dean was a third-round pick (2022) who developed into a starting LB, posting a career-high 128 tackles in 2024 and 55 tackles with four sacks in 10 games in 2025. His departure is partially mitigated by second-year LB Jihaad Campbell, who was drafted as Dean's heir apparent and is expected to step into a larger role.

Blankenship was an undrafted free agent success story who started 50 games for Philadelphia and was named a team captain in 2025. His departure leaves a major void at starting safety. The current internal candidates — Drew Mukuba (returning from injury), Sydney Brown, Andre Sam, and Michael Carter II — represent a significant step down in reliability.

Dotson was acquired in a 2024 trade with Washington and served as the Eagles' WR3 in 2025. His departure thins the receiver depth behind A.J. Brown and DeVonta Smith (assuming Brown remains). With A.J. Brown's trade status uncertain, WR depth is now a notable concern.

Jackson started at outside cornerback in 2025, with Cooper DeJean moving to nickel. His departure (if he doesn't re-sign) would have been the primary CB2 void. The Eagles signing Riq Woolen addresses this directly. Jackson's status as of March 11 is unsigned/undecided, but Woolen's addition makes his return a depth question rather than a need.

Goedert broke the Eagles franchise TE record with 11 TD grabs in 2025. As of March 11, he has not signed elsewhere and talks with the Eagles are reportedly ongoing, though he is widely expected to test the market. If he leaves, the Eagles take on roughly $20M in dead cap and are left with a massive void at TE1. The signings of Mundt and Calcaterra are complementary pieces only — neither is a TE1 replacement.
Pryor was a reserve guard/tackle who played 17 games (1 start) for Philadelphia in 2025. Depth-level loss with minimal impact on the need picture.

Graham came out of retirement to play nine games for the Eagles in 2025. He has expressed openness to an 18th season and Roseman has said he is welcome back. As of March 11 his status is undecided, but even if he returns it would be as a veteran presence/mentor, not a solution to the EDGE starter void.

signings.analysis: The Eagles locked down their nose tackle to a 3-year, $78M extension ($65M guaranteed) — making Davis the highest-paid nose tackle in NFL history and the second-highest-paid IDL behind only Chris Jones. Coming off a career year (72 tackles, 9 TFL, 4.5 sacks, 22 pressures), this is a full long-term commitment through 2029. IDL is no longer a need — Davis and Jalen Carter (still on rookie deal) form one of the best interior DL duos in the league. This deal removes IDL entirely from the draft needs list.

Woolen fills the CB2/outside corner vacancy left by Adoree' Jackson's departure. The addition gives the Eagles three high-upside corners — Mitchell (All-Pro), DeJean (All-Pro), and Woolen (Pro Bowl 2022, 12 career INTs) — which is a legitimate strength. However, it is a one-year prove-it deal, worth up to $15M including incentives. Woolen's role decreased in Seattle in 2025 (7 starts in 16 games) and this is structured as a one-year showcase rather than a long-term solution. CB remains a moderate need for the 2027 horizon, but the 2026 starting unit is addressed. The Eagles may still want to draft a developmental CB. Need drops from critical/Tier 1 to moderate/Tier 3.

Mundt is a blocking-specialist TE who fills a specific void the Eagles had in 2025 — a lack of a true inline blocker in the tight end room. He has familiarity with new OC Sean Mannion (both were with the Rams) and new OL coach Chris Kuper (Vikings). Nine catches for 111 yards in 2025 underscore that he is not a receiving threat. This is a complementary signing that addresses a scheme fit, not the TE1 starter problem. If Goedert walks, the TE need remains critical. One-year deal at depth value.

Calcaterra returns for a fifth season as the Eagles' pass-catching TE2 option behind Goedert. He has 42 career catches for 494 yards and 2 TDs in 62 games — a reliable but unspectacular complementary piece. His best season came in 2024 when Goedert missed games. Re-signing Calcaterra on a one-year deal keeps roster continuity but does nothing to address the TE1 hole if Goedert leaves. Minimal impact on the need picture.

Carter's original cap hit was over $10.1M for 2026 — a problematic number given his limited usage (106 defensive snaps, 65 special teams snaps in 2025). The Eagles renegotiated the deal downward to free cap space while retaining a versatile DB who can play nickel corner or safety. He may compete for the starting safety job post-Blankenship, but he is not a proven starter at S. This is a cap-management move with a depth floor, not a need-solving move. S remains a prominent need entering the draft.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: Jordan Davis extension (3yr/$78M/$65M guaranteed through 2029) locks up the best nose tackle in the NFL. Paired with Jalen Carter on his rookie deal, this is a dominant IDL duo. Remove from draft needs entirely. Day 3 depth only if they want developmental rotational pieces.

Jaelan Phillips departed on a 4yr/$120M deal to Carolina. Only Nolan Smith Jr. and Jalyx Hunt remain as active-roster EDGE players under contract. No meaningful free agent addition made at the position. This is now the Eagles' most critical need heading into the draft — a Tier 1 priority. Should target EDGE in rounds 1-2.

Reed Blankenship signed a 3yr/$24.75M deal with the Texans. The Eagles have no proven starting safety on the roster. Drew Mukuba (2025 2nd-round pick) is returning from injury and is the likely starter, but an opposite-side starter is urgently needed. Michael Carter II (renegotiated, depth/nickel) is not the answer. Safety is a co-equal Tier 1 need with EDGE entering the draft — should address rounds 2-4.

Nakobe Dean signed a 3yr/$36M deal with the Raiders. Jihaad Campbell provides an internal solution at LB and is expected to step into a larger role, which prevents this from becoming Tier 1. However, the position does need attention — Campbell is still developing and the Eagles lack proven LB depth. Tier 2 need; rounds 3-5 range.

Dallas Goedert remains unsigned and is widely expected to leave. His departure would trigger ~$20M in dead cap and leave the Eagles with only Mundt (blocking specialist, 1yr) and Calcaterra (pass-catcher, 1yr) as TE options — neither is a TE1. With the new Shanahan-McVay scheme under OC Sean Mannion, a receiving/versatile TE is critical. If Goedert departs (likely), TE escalates to Tier 1. This must be addressed in free agency or rounds 2-3 of the draft. Note: Goedert's situation is still fluid as of March 11 — downgrade only if he formally signs elsewhere.

Riq Woolen signing (1yr/up to $15M) fills the CB2 starting role vacated by Adoree' Jackson. With Mitchell, DeJean, and Woolen, the 2026 starting CB unit is legitimate. However, Woolen's one-year deal means this need resurfaces in 2027. Eagles should target a developmental CB in rounds 3-5 to groom as a future starter, but this is no longer a priority pick.

Jahan Dotson (WR3) departed to Atlanta. The A.J. Brown trade situation remains unresolved — if Brown is dealt, WR becomes a Tier 1 emergency. Even assuming Brown stays, WR depth is thin behind Brown and DeVonta Smith. No WR additions made in free agency. Need remains Tier 2 entering the draft; escalates to Tier 1 if Brown is traded.

key_additions.headline: Career-year nose tackle locked up as highest-paid NT in NFL history, anchoring a dominant DL duo with Jalen Carter
Pro Bowl corner and Super Bowl champion fills the CB2 void, giving Philly three elite corners alongside Mitchell and DeJean
Blocking TE with Shanahan-scheme familiarity addresses Eagles' 2025 run-game weakness at tight end
Pass-catching TE2 returns for a fifth season, providing continuity in a rebuilding tight end room

key_additions.contract_summary: Renegotiated deal (terms undisclosed, reduced from $10.1M cap hit)
key_additions.headline: Versatile DB retained at reduced cap cost to provide secondary depth at nickel and safety

key_losses.headline: Eagles' best pass rusher departs on a blockbuster deal, leaving Nolan Smith and Jalyx Hunt as the only edge rushers under contract
50-game starter and team captain walks, leaving a gaping hole at starting safety alongside second-year Drew Mukuba
Starting LB departs after four seasons; Jihaad Campbell is the in-house heir apparent but the position needs depth
WR3 leaves for Atlanta, thinning receiver depth behind Brown and Smith amid A.J. Brown trade uncertainty

key_losses.contract_summary: TBD — still unsigned as of March 11
key_losses.headline: Franchise-record 11-TD tight end remains unsigned and expected to depart; exit would create Tier 1 need and ~$20M dead cap hit

notes: Dallas Goedert and Adoree' Jackson remain unsigned as of March 11, 2026 (official new league year start). Both are classified as potential departures pending official action.

Contract guarantees for Blankenship's Texans deal, Mundt's Eagles deal, and Calcaterra's Eagles deal not yet fully reported. Blankenship AAV/structure sourced from NFL Network/NBC Sports Philadelphia; Mundt and Calcaterra terms are one-year deals per NFL Network/ESPN but dollar values not yet disclosed.
Michael Carter II renegotiated contract terms not fully disclosed — original cap hit was $10.1M; reduced amount not confirmed.
A.J. Brown trade situation is unresolved and could dramatically shift the WR need if a deal is executed.
Jalen Carter extension eligible but not yet executed — his extension would further cement the IDL need as resolved for years.
Eagles received four 2026 compensatory picks (R3/98, R4/137, R5/178, R6/215) for 2025 FA losses (Becton, Rodgers, Sweat, M. Williams). Total 2026 picks: 9.
